overview: LAN Architecture covers campus design tiers (2-tier vs. 3-tier) and Power over Ethernet (PoE) standards used to power access-layer devices like IP phones and APs.
definitions: 2-tier (collapsed core) design: star topology at access, partial mesh at distribution; the core function is collapsed into the distribution layer.
3-tier design: distribution switches connect up to a dedicated core layer.
PSE: Power Sourcing Equipment (e.g. the switch supplying power).
PD: Powered Device (e.g. an IP phone or AP receiving power).
core_concepts: Choose 2-tier for smaller campuses (simpler, cheaper); choose 3-tier when scale requires a dedicated high-speed core.
best_practices: Size PoE budgets per switch based on the actual devices connected (phone vs. Wi-Fi 6 AP can have very different power needs).
interview_questions: When would you choose a 2-tier design over a 3-tier design?
What is the difference between PSE and PD?
Compare the power budgets of 802.3af, 802.3at, and 802.3bt.
exam_traps: Assuming all PoE standards supply the same wattage — af/at/bt differ significantly (15.4W / 30W / 60-100W).
enterprise_notes: PoE-powered APs and IP phones are also the classic real-world use case driving QoS voice/video prioritization.
